## rename test1 to il6 and test2 to crp
pt <- rename(pt, il6 = test1, crp = test2)
## Not run due to redundancy !!!
## Check the data
codebook(pt)

2.7.4 arrange

2.8 append

to add this after introducing select and generate

Often the data are collected in multiple sites and datasets are split into several files. We use append function to add more observations of the same variables. To use append, the datasets must have the same names of variables in the same column.

Let us first read the second dataset into R.

## read second dataset
pt2 <- haven::read_dta("https://stats.idre.ucla.edu/stat/data/patient_pt2_stata_dm.dta")
## check data
codebook(pt2)
#> ______________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#> Codebook: 'pt2' 
#> ______________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>  No     Variable |        Label      Type Obs_Num <NA> <NA>(%)
#>  -- ------------ + ------------ --------- ------- ---- -------
#>   1     hospital |         <NA> character     111    0       0
#>   2       hospid |          HID   numeric     111    0       0
#>   3        docid |         <NA> character     111    0       0
#>   4     dis_date |         <NA>      Date     111    0       0
#>   5    tumorsize |         <NA>   numeric     111    0       0
#>   6          co2 |         <NA>   numeric     111    0       0
#>   7         pain |         <NA>   numeric     111    0       0
#>   8        wound |         <NA>   numeric     111    0       0
#>   9     mobility |         <NA>   numeric     111    0       0
#>  10      ntumors |         <NA>   numeric     111    0       0
#>  11    remission |         <NA>   numeric     111    0       0
#>  12 lungcapacity |         <NA>   numeric     111    0       0
#>  13          age |          Age   numeric     111    0       0
#>  14      married |      Married   numeric     111    0       0
#>  15     familyhx |     FamilyHx character     111    0       0
#>  16    smokinghx |    SmokingHx character     111    0       0
#>  17          sex |          Sex character     111    0       0
#>  18  cancerstage |  CancerStage character     111    0       0
#>  19 lengthofstay | LengthofStay   numeric     111    0       0
#>  20          wbc |          WBC character     111    0       0
#>  21          rbc |          RBC   numeric     111    0       0
#>  22          bmi |          BMI   numeric     111    0       0
#>  23        test1 |          IL6   numeric     111    0       0
#>  24        test2 |          CRP   numeric     111    0       0
#>  -- ------------ + ------------ --------- ------- ---- -------
#> ______________________________________________________________

In this second dataset, there are only 24 variables

2.9 Dates

how to create today’s date use system date and time etc. day, month, year formatDate is.Date

2.10 expand2, countBy, lagRows

2.11 reshaping data

3 Data Analysis

3.1 Exploratory Data Analysis

3.1.1 pyramid

3.2 Epidemiological Measures

3.2.1 Creating epi dataset

3.2.1.1 expandFreq, expandTables

3.2.2 Odds Ratio

3.2.3 Risk Ratio

3.2.4 Rate Ratio

3.3 Linear Regression

This chapter covers a variety of topics about using R for linear regression. We demonstrate how R can be used for linear regression analysis. This chapter is based on the UCLA’s STATA web tutorial. 2

In this chapter, we use a dataset that was created by random sampling of 400 elementary schools from API 2000 dataset of the United States’ California Department of Education. This is the same dataset from the tutorial.

Let’s import this dataset to R.

elemapi <- haven::read_dta("https://stats.idre.ucla.edu/stat/stata/webbooks/reg/elemapi.dta")

3.3.1 First regression analysis

Now let’s run our first regression analysis. We focus on one dependent variable, api00 and three independent variables, acs_k3, meals, and full.

api00 - the academic performance of the school 
acs_k3 - the average class size in kindergarten through third grade
meals - the percentage of students receiving free meals
full - the percentage of teachers who have full teaching credentials

The meals is an indicator of poverty. We hypothesize that better academic performance is associated with the lower class size, fewer students receiving free meals, and a higher proportion of teachers with full teaching credentials. Below is the R command to fit and generate regression analysis.

## Fit the regression model using lm
fit <- lm(api00 ~ acs_k3 + meals + full, data = elemapi)

## Generate regression output 
regress(fit)
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>                 Linear Regression Output 
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>          Stats |     Value              Source |      DF         SS        MS
#>  ------------- + ---------          ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>  Number of Obs |       313               Model |       3  2634884.3  878294.8
#>      F(3, 309) |    213.41            Residual |     309  1271713.2    4115.6
#>       Prob > F |   0.00000          ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>      R-Squared |   0.67447               Total |     312  3906597.5   12521.1
#>  Adj R-Squared |   0.67131                                                   
#>            AIC |   3499.18                                 Root MSE     64.15
#>                |                                                             
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>          api00 |     Coef.  Std.Err            t   P>|t| [95% Conf. Interval]
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>         acs_k3 |  -2.68151  1.39399        -1.92 0.05532   -5.41373   0.05071
#>          meals |  -3.70242  0.15403       -24.04 0.00000   -4.00431  -3.40053
#>           full |   0.10861  0.09072         1.20 0.23214   -0.06920   0.28642
#>    (Intercept) | 906.73916 28.26505        32.08 0.00000  851.33967 962.13865
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> (Fit: lm(formula = api00 ~ acs_k3 + meals + full, data = elemapi))
#> (acs_k3: avg class size k-3)
#> (meals: pct free meals)
#> (full: pct full credential)
#> (api00: api 2000)

Of the three independent variables, acs_k3 and meals have a negative relationship with api00, meaning that a larger class size or higher number of students having free meals is associated with lower academic performance. However, acs_k3 does not show a statistically significant association as the p-value is greater than 0.05. Moreover, meals is highly related to income level and serves as a proxy for poverty.

Finally, full has a mild positive relationship, showing that one unit increase in the percentage of teachers with full teaching credentials, only 0.11 unit increase in academic performance. However, the relationship is not statistically significant.

Before we publish these results, we need to run several checks to make sure so we can firmly stand behind these results.

3.3.2 Examining the dataset

We start by having a close look at the dataset.

## Check the dataset
codebook(elemapi)
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#> Codebook: 'elemapi' 
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>  No Variable |                             Label    Type Obs_Num <NA> <NA>(%)
#>  -- -------- + --------------------------------- ------- ------- ---- -------
#>   1     snum |                     school number numeric     400    0       0
#>   2     dnum |                   district number numeric     400    0       0
#>   3    api00 |                          api 2000 numeric     400    0       0
#>   4    api99 |                          api 1999 numeric     400    0       0
#>   5   growth |               growth 1999 to 2000 numeric     400    0       0
#>   6    meals |                    pct free meals numeric     315   85    21.2
#>   7      ell |         english language learners numeric     400    0       0
#>   8   yr_rnd |                 year round school  double     400    0       0
#>   9 mobility |            pct 1st year in school numeric     399    1     0.2
#>  10   acs_k3 |                avg class size k-3 numeric     398    2     0.5
#>  11   acs_46 |                avg class size 4-6 numeric     397    3     0.8
#>  12  not_hsg |                    parent not hsg numeric     400    0       0
#>  13      hsg |                        parent hsg numeric     400    0       0
#>  14 some_col |               parent some college numeric     400    0       0
#>  15 col_grad |               parent college grad numeric     400    0       0
#>  16 grad_sch |                parent grad school numeric     400    0       0
#>  17   avg_ed |                     avg parent ed numeric     381   19     4.8
#>  18     full |               pct full credential numeric     400    0       0
#>  19     emer |               pct emer credential numeric     400    0       0
#>  20   enroll |                number of students numeric     400    0       0
#>  21  mealcat | Percentage free meals in 3 cat...  double     400    0       0
#>  -- -------- + --------------------------------- ------- ------- ---- -------
#> _____________________________________________________________________________

There are 400 observations and 21 variables. Our regression analysis only used 313 observations. The meals indicates a substantial amount (21%) of missing data, followed by avg_ed with 4.8%.

There are several variables about school performance, class size, parent education, percentages of teachers, and number of students.

## Summary measures of four variables 
summ(elemapi, api00, acs_k3, meals, full)
#> ____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#> Summary 
#> ____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>  Variables | Obs. NA.  Mean Std.Dev Median    Q1    Q3   Min   Max Normality
#>  --------- + ---- --- ----- ------- ------ ----- ----- ----- ----- ---------
#>      api00 |  400   0 647.6   142.2  643.0 523.8 762.2 369.0 940.0     0.000
#>     acs_k3 |  398   2  18.5     5.0   19.0  18.0  20.0 -21.0  25.0     0.000
#>      meals |  315  85  72.0    24.4   77.0  57.0  93.0   6.0 100.0     0.000
#>       full |  400   0  66.1    40.3   87.0   0.9  97.0   0.4 100.0     0.000
#>  --------- + ---- --- ----- ------- ------ ----- ----- ----- ----- ---------
#> ____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> (api00: api 2000)
#> (acs_k3: avg class size k-3)
#> (meals: pct free meals)
#> (full: pct full credential)

It seems that some class sizes show negative values which may be data entry error and percentages of teachers also range from very low value 0.4 to full percentage, 100.

Let’s first tabulate the class size.

## Tabulation of acs_k3 
tab(elemapi, acs_k3)
#> ________________________________________ 
#> 
#> One-way Tabulation 
#> ________________________________________ 
#> 
#>  Variables Category | Freq Percent  Cum.
#>  --------- -------- + ---- ------- -----
#>     acs_k3      -21 |    3     0.8   0.8
#>                 -20 |    2     0.5   1.3
#>                 -19 |    1     0.2   1.5
#>                  14 |    2     0.5   2.0
#>                  15 |    1     0.2   2.2
#>                  16 |   14     3.5   5.7
#>                  17 |   20     5.0  10.7
#>                  18 |   64    16.0  26.7
#>                  19 |  143    35.8  62.5
#>                  20 |   97    24.2  86.7
#>                  21 |   40    10.0  96.7
#>                  22 |    7     1.8  98.5
#>                  23 |    3     0.8  99.3
#>                  25 |    1     0.2  99.5
#>                <NA> |    2     0.5 100.0
#>  --------- -------- | ---- ------- -----
#>               Total |  400     100   100
#>  --------- -------- + ---- ------- -----
#> ________________________________________ 
#> (acs_k3: avg class size k-3)

Next, we look at the school and district number of these negative values in acs_k3.

## Load packages 
library(dplyr)
library(magrittr)

## we check those school and district if acs_k3 is negative
elemapi %>% 
  filter(acs_k3 < 0) %>%
  tab(snum, dnum)
#> ________________________________________ 
#> 
#> One-way Tabulation 
#> ________________________________________ 
#> 
#>  Variables Category | Freq Percent  Cum.
#>  --------- -------- + ---- ------- -----
#>       snum      592 |    1    16.7  16.7
#>                 595 |    1    16.7  33.4
#>                 596 |    1    16.7  50.1
#>                 600 |    1    16.7  66.8
#>                 602 |    1    16.7  83.5
#>                 611 |    1    16.7 100.2
#>       ----    ----- | ---- ------- -----
#>       dnum      140 |    6   100.0 100.0
#>  --------- -------- | ---- ------- -----
#>               Total |    6     100   100
#>  --------- -------- + ---- ------- -----
#> ________________________________________ 
#> (snum: school number)
#> (dnum: district number)

All observations with negative acs_k3 come from the district number, 140. let’s list all observations in that district.

## we check all observations for district 140
elemapi %>% 
  filter(dnum == 140) %>% 
  select(dnum, snum, api00, acs_k3, meals, full)
#> # A tibble: 6 x 6
#>    dnum  snum api00 acs_k3 meals  full
#>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
#> 1   140   600   843    -20    NA    91
#> 2   140   596   800    -19    NA    94
#> 3   140   611   857    -20    NA   100
#> 4   140   595   713    -21    63    92
#> 5   140   592   804    -21    NA    97
#> # ... with 1 more row

It seems all observations of acs_k3 from the district 140 have the same problem. Make a note about this and continue with checking the dataset.

Below we look at the histogram of acs_k3 and a boxplot to show this.

## Set up graphical parameters 
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
## Histogram with normal curve overlay 
histogram(elemapi, acs_k3)
#> (A histogram is drawn for 'acs_k3' with overlay normal curve)

## We use %$% as boxplot() does not have data input.
elemapi %$% 
  boxplot(acs_k3, main = "Boxplot of 'acs_k3'", 
          xlab = "Average class size")


## Reset graphical parameters 
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

You can see the outliers on the left side of the histogram and at the bottom of the boxplot.

Now we check the variable full whether very low percentages are valid. Below we tabulate if full is less than 50%.

## Tabulate if full < 50.0
elemapi %>% 
  filter(full < 50.0) %>% 
  tab(full)
#> _________________________________________________ 
#> 
#> One-way Tabulation 
#> _________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>  Variables          Category | Freq Percent  Cum.
#>  --------- ----------------- + ---- ------- -----
#>       full 0.419999986886978 |    1     0.9   0.9
#>            0.449999988079071 |    1     0.9   1.8
#>             0.46000000834465 |    1     0.9   2.7
#>            0.469999998807907 |    1     0.9   3.6
#>            0.479999989271164 |    1     0.9   4.5
#>                          0.5 |    3     2.7   7.2
#>            0.509999990463257 |    1     0.9   8.1
#>            0.519999980926514 |    1     0.9   9.0
#>            0.529999971389771 |    1     0.9   9.9
#>            0.540000021457672 |    1     0.9  10.8
#>            0.560000002384186 |    2     1.8  12.6
#>            0.569999992847443 |    2     1.8  14.4
#>            0.579999983310699 |    1     0.9  15.3
#>            0.589999973773956 |    3     2.7  18.0
#>            0.600000023841858 |    1     0.9  18.9
#>            0.610000014305115 |    4     3.6  22.5
#>            0.620000004768372 |    2     1.8  24.3
#>            0.629999995231628 |    1     0.9  25.2
#>            0.639999985694885 |    3     2.7  27.9
#>            0.649999976158142 |    3     2.7  30.6
#>            0.660000026226044 |    2     1.8  32.4
#>            0.670000016689301 |    6     5.4  37.8
#>            0.680000007152557 |    2     1.8  39.6
#>            0.689999997615814 |    3     2.7  42.3
#>            0.699999988079071 |    1     0.9  43.2
#>            0.709999978542328 |    1     0.9  44.1
#>            0.720000028610229 |    2     1.8  45.9
#>            0.730000019073486 |    6     5.4  51.3
#>                         0.75 |    4     3.6  54.9
#>            0.759999990463257 |    2     1.8  56.7
#>            0.769999980926514 |    2     1.8  58.5
#>            0.790000021457672 |    3     2.7  61.2
#>            0.800000011920929 |    5     4.5  65.7
#>            0.810000002384186 |    8     7.1  72.8
#>            0.819999992847443 |    2     1.8  74.6
#>            0.829999983310699 |    2     1.8  76.4
#>            0.839999973773956 |    2     1.8  78.2
#>            0.850000023841858 |    3     2.7  80.9
#>            0.860000014305115 |    2     1.8  82.7
#>            0.899999976158142 |    3     2.7  85.4
#>            0.920000016689301 |    1     0.9  86.3
#>            0.930000007152557 |    1     0.9  87.2
#>            0.939999997615814 |    2     1.8  89.0
#>            0.949999988079071 |    2     1.8  90.8
#>            0.959999978542328 |    1     0.9  91.7
#>                            1 |    2     1.8  93.5
#>                           37 |    1     0.9  94.4
#>                           41 |    1     0.9  95.3
#>                           44 |    2     1.8  97.1
#>                           45 |    2     1.8  98.9
#>                           46 |    1     0.9  99.8
#>                           48 |    1     0.9 100.7
#>  --------- ----------------- | ---- ------- -----
#>                        Total |  112     100   100
#>  --------- ----------------- + ---- ------- -----
#> _________________________________________________ 
#> (full: pct full credential)

It seems like they are recorded as proportions instead of percentages.

Let’s check which district they come from.

## Check district number 
elemapi %>% 
  filter(full < 37) %>% 
  tab(dnum)
#> ________________________________________ 
#> 
#> One-way Tabulation 
#> ________________________________________ 
#> 
#>  Variables Category | Freq Percent  Cum.
#>  --------- -------- + ---- ------- -----
#>       dnum      401 |  104   100.0 100.0
#>  --------- -------- | ---- ------- -----
#>               Total |  104     100   100
#>  --------- -------- + ---- ------- -----
#> ________________________________________ 
#> (dnum: district number)

We have 104 such observations that come from the district number 401.

Another useful technique is to use a scatterplot matrix. While it is useful as a data screening tool, it is commonly used as a diagnostic tool for non-linearities and outliers of your dataset.

## Scatterplot matrix on 4 variables
elemapi %>% 
  select(api00, acs_k3, meals, full) %>% 
  scatterPlotMatrix()

So far, we have identified three problems while looking at four variables in the dataset elemapi.

1) A large number of missing values in `meals`
2) Negative values in `acs_k3`
3) Miscoded percentage values in `full`

Now let’s use the correct version and re-run our regression analysis.

## Import elemapi - corrected version
elemapi2 <- haven::read_dta(
  "https://stats.idre.ucla.edu/stat/stata/webbooks/reg/elemapi2.dta"
)
## Fit linear model: Note we use 'elemapi2'
fit <- lm(api00 ~ acs_k3 + meals + full, data = elemapi2)

## Generate regression output 
regress(fit)
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>                 Linear Regression Output 
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>          Stats |     Value              Source |      DF         SS        MS
#>  ------------- + ---------          ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>  Number of Obs |       398               Model |       3  6604966.2 2201655.4
#>      F(3, 394) |    615.55            Residual |     394  1409241.0    3576.8
#>       Prob > F |   0.00000          ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>      R-Squared |   0.82416               Total |     397  8014207.1   20186.9
#>  Adj R-Squared |   0.82282                                                   
#>            AIC |   4391.97                                 Root MSE     59.81
#>                |                                                             
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>          api00 |     Coef.  Std.Err            t   P>|t| [95% Conf. Interval]
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>         acs_k3 |  -0.71706  2.23882        -0.32 0.74892   -5.10515   3.67103
#>          meals |  -3.68626  0.11178       -32.98 0.00000   -3.90535  -3.46718
#>           full |   1.32714  0.23887         5.56 0.00000    0.85895   1.79533
#>    (Intercept) | 771.65811 48.86071        15.79 0.00000  675.89111 867.42510
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> (Fit: lm(formula = api00 ~ acs_k3 + meals + full, data = elemapi2))
#> (acs_k3: avg class size k-3)
#> (meals: pct free meals)
#> (full: pct full credential)
#> (api00: api 2000)

We have covered data checking before regression analysis.

3.3.3 Simple Linear Regression

Let’s take a step back and we look at simple linear regression (SLR). SLR has only one independent (predictor) variable. It can be either numeric or categorical. SLR’s response (outcome) variable must be numeric.

In R, regress is used to generate the regression output after fitting the linear model using either lm() or glm() function.

Below we fit the linear model to examine the relationship between the size of the school, enroll, and academic performance, api00.

## Fit linear model: Note we use 'elemapi2'
fit <- lm(api00 ~ enroll, data = elemapi2)

## Generate regression output 
regress(fit)

We use the same object fit to assign the output from a fitting linear model. You do not need to assign to different names as we run the command from top to bottom and we do not plan to use earlier fit versions.

3.3.3.1 Annotated output

The regression output is shown below with annotated explanations.

Annotated Output of Linear Regression

Let’s review the regression output.

  • We see all 400 observations are used in the analysis.

  • The F-test is statistically significant, which means that the model is statistically significant.

  • The R-squared of .1012 means that approximately 10% of the variance of api00 is accounted for by the model, in this case, enroll. The t-test for enroll equals -6.70, and is statistically significant, meaning that the regression coefficient for enroll is significantly different from zero. Note that (-6.70)^2 = 44.89, which is the same as the F-statistic (with some rounding error).

  • The coefficient for enroll is -.1998674, or approximately -.2, meaning that for one unit increase in enroll, we would expect a .2-unit decrease in api00. In other words, a school with 1100 students would be expected to have an API score 20 units lower than a school with 1000 students.

  • The constant or Intercept is 744.2514, and this is the predicted value when enroll equals zero. In most cases, this is not very interesting.

    The output has four parts:

      1) Statistics about the model in the blue box, 
  2) Variance Table in the red box, and 
  3) The coefficient table in the green box. 
  4) Regression formula with variable labels in the yellow box

Below the tables, the fitting formula is displayed along with variable labels.

Footnotes3

a. Number of Obs

This is the number of observations used in the regression analysis.

b & c. F Value

The F Value is the Mean Square Model (817326.293) divided by the Mean Square Residual (18232.0244), yielding F=44.83. The p-value associated with this F value is very small (0.0000). These values are used to answer the question “Do the independent variables reliably predict the dependent variable?”. The p-value is compared to your alpha level (typically 0.05) and, if smaller, you can conclude “Yes, the independent variables reliably predict the dependent variable”. You could say that the variable enroll can be used to reliably predict api00 (the dependent variable). If the p-value were greater than 0.05, you would say that the independent variable does not show a significant relationship with the dependent variable, or that the independent variable does not reliably predict the dependent variable. b. c. 

d. R-Squared

It is the proportion of variance in the dependent variable (api00) which can be predicted from the independent variable (enroll). This value indicates that 10% of the variance in api00 can be predicted from the variable enroll.

e. Adjusted R-squared

As predictors are added to the model, each predictor will explain some of the variances in the dependent variable simply due to chance. One could continue to add predictors to the model which would continue to improve the ability of the predictors to explain the dependent variable, although some of this increase in R-squared would be simply due to chance variation in that particular sample. The adjusted R-Squared attempts to yield a more honest value to estimate the R-squared for the population. The value of R-Squared was .10, while the value of Adjusted R-Squared was .099. Adjusted R-squared is computed using the formula 1 – ( (1-Rsq)*(N-1)/(N-k-1) ). From this formula, you can see that when the number of observations is small and the number of predictors is large, there will be a much greater difference between R-Squared and adjusted R-Squared, because the ratio (N-1)/(N-k-1) will be much greater than 1 and adjusted R-squared will be much smaller than unadjusted R-squared. By contrast, when the number of observations is very large compared to the number of predictors, the value of R-square and adjusted R-square will be much closer because the ratio (N-1)/(N-k-1) will approach 1.

f. AIC Value

The AIC (Akaike information criterion) is used as a measure of model parsimony. It penalizes the likelihood by the number of parameters to add an amount to it that is proportional to the number of parameters. Lower indicates a more parsimonious model, relative to a model fit with a higher AIC. However, the comparisons are only valid for models that are fit to the same response data, i.e., values of y.

g. Root MSE

The Root MSE is the standard deviation of the error term, and is the square root of the Mean Square Residuals (or Errors).

h. Source

This is the source of variance, Model, Residual, and Total. The Total variance is partitioned into the variance which can be explained by the independent variables (Model) and the variance which is not explained by the independent variables. Note that the Sums of Squares for the Model and Residual add up to the Total Variance, reflecting the fact that the Total Variance is partitioned into Model and Residual variance.

i. DF

These are the Sum of Squares associated with the three sources of variance, Total, Model & Residual. These can be computed in many ways. Conceptually, these formulas can be expressed as: SSTotal. The total variability around the mean. Σ(Y – Ybar)^2. SSResidual. The sum of squared errors in prediction. Σ(Y – Ypredicted)^2 . SSModel. The improvement in prediction by using the predicted value of Y over just using the mean of Y. Hence, this would be the squared differences between the predicted value of Y and the mean of Y, Σ(Ypredicted – Ybar)^2. Another way to think of this is the SSModel is SSTotal – SSResidual. Note that the SSTotal = SSModel + SSResidual. Note that SSModel / SSTotal is equal to .10, the value of R-Square. This is because R-Square is the proportion of the variance explained by the independent variables, hence can be computed by SSModel / SSTotal.

j. SS

These are the degrees of freedom associated with the sources of variance. The total variance has N-1 degrees of freedom. In this case, there were N=400 observations, so the DF for total is 399. The model degrees of freedom corresponds to the number of predictors minus 1 (K-1). You may think this would be 1-1 (since there was 1 independent variable in the model statement, enroll). But, the intercept is automatically included in the model (unless you explicitly omit the intercept). Including the intercept, there are 2 predictors, so the model has 2-1=1 degree of freedom. The Residual degree of freedom is the DF total minus the DF model, 399 – 1 is 398.

k. MS

These are the Mean Squares, the Sum of Squares divided by their respective DF. For the Model, 817326.293 / 1 is equal to 817326.293. For the Residual, 7256345.7 / 398 equals 18232.0244. These are computed so you can compute the F ratio, dividing the Mean Square Model by the Mean Square Residual to test the significance of the predictor(s) in the model.

l. api00

This column shows the dependent variable at the top (api00) with the predictor variables below it (enroll). The last variable (Intercept) represents the constant, also referred to in textbooks as the Y-intercept, the height of the regression line when it crosses the Y-axis.

m. Coef.

These are the values for the regression equation for predicting the dependent variable from the independent variable. The regression equation is presented in many different ways, for example…

`Ypredicted` = `b0` + `b1` * `x1`

The column of estimates (coefficients or parameter estimates, from here on labeled coefficients) provides the values for b0 and b1 for this equation. Expressed in terms of the variables used in this example, the regression equation is

`api00Predicted` = `744.25` – `.20` * `enroll`

This estimate tells you about the relationship between the independent variable and the dependent variable. This estimate indicates the amount of increase in api00 that would be predicted by a 1 unit increase in the predictor.

Note: If an independent variable is not significant, the coefficient is not significantly different from 0, which should be taken into account when interpreting the coefficient. (See the columns with the t value and p-value about testing whether the coefficients are significant). enroll – The coefficient (parameter estimate) is -.20. So, for every unit increase in enroll, a -.20 unit decrease in api00 is predicted.

n. Standard Errors

These are the standard errors associated with the coefficients. The standard error is used for testing whether the parameter is significantly different from 0 by dividing the parameter estimate by the standard error to obtain a t value (see the column with t values and p values). The standard errors can also be used to form a confidence interval for the parameter, as shown in the last 2 columns of this table.

o & p. t and p Values

These columns provide the t value and 2 tailed p value used in testing the null hypothesis that the coefficient/parameter is 0. If you use a 2 tailed test, then you would compare each p-value to your pre-selected value of alpha. Coefficients having p values less than alpha are significant. For example, if you chose alpha to be 0.05, coefficients having a p-value of 0.05 or less would be statistically significant (i.e. you can reject the null hypothesis and say that the coefficient is significantly different from 0). If you use a 1 tailed test (i.e., you predict that the parameter will go in a particular direction), then you can divide the p-value by 2 before comparing it to your preselected alpha level. With a 2 tailed test and alpha of 0.05, you can reject the null hypothesis that the coefficient for enroll is equal to 0. The coefficient of -.20 is significantly different from 0. The constant (Intercept) is significantly different from 0 at the 0.05 alpha level. However, having a significant intercept is seldom interesting.

q. 95% Confident Interval

This shows a 95% confidence interval for the coefficient. This is very useful as it helps you understand how high and how low the actual population value of the parameter might be. Such confidence intervals help you to put the estimate from the coefficient into perspective by seeing how much the value could vary.

3.3.3.2 Fitted values

In addition to getting the regression output, it can be useful to see a scatterplot of the predicted and outcome variables with the regression line plotted. After you run regress, you can use the predict function to generate predicted (fitted) values. You can get these values at any point by inputting the output from regress.

Below we predict the output from regress.

## To avoid displaying the output from regress 
sink <- capture.output(lm <- regress(fit)) 

## Output from regress is stored in lm. Let's predict lm
predict(lm) %>% 
  ## check the dataset
  codebook
#> ______________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#> Codebook: '.' 
#> ______________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>  No  Variable |                             Label    Type Obs_Num <NA> <NA>(%)
#>  -- --------- + --------------------------------- ------- ------- ---- -------
#>   1     api00 |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>   2    enroll |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>   3      snum |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>   4      dnum |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>   5     api99 |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>   6    growth |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>   7     meals |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>   8       ell |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>   9    yr_rnd |                 year round school  double     402    0       0
#>  10  mobility |                              <NA> numeric     401    1     0.2
#>  11    acs_k3 |                              <NA> numeric     400    2     0.5
#>  12    acs_46 |                              <NA> numeric     399    3     0.7
#>  13   not_hsg |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>  14       hsg |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>  15  some_col |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>  16  col_grad |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>  17  grad_sch |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>  18    avg_ed |                              <NA> numeric     383   19     4.7
#>  19      full |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>  20      emer |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>  21   mealcat | Percentage free meals in 3 cat...  double     402    0       0
#>  22   collcat |                              <NA> numeric     402    0       0
#>  23    fitted |                     Fitted values numeric     402    0       0
#>  24     resid |                         Residuals numeric     402    0       0
#>  25 std.resid |             Studentized Residuals numeric     402    0       0
#>  26       hat |            Leverage or hat values numeric     402    0       0
#>  27     sigma |                         hat sigma numeric     402    0       0
#>  28    cooksd |                   Cook's Distance numeric     402    0       0
#>  -- --------- + --------------------------------- ------- ------- ---- -------
#> ______________________________________________________________________________

3.3.3.3 Scatter plots

Below we create a scatterplot of the outcome variable, api00 and the predictor, enroll.

## Plot api00 and enroll 
plot(api00 ~ enroll, data = elemapi2, 
     main = "Scatter plot with regression line", 
     xlab = "Number of students",
     ylab = "Academic Performance")

## Add regression line 
abline(fit, col = "red")

3.3.4 Multiple Linear Regression

Multiple linear regression (MLR) has one outcome variable and multiple predictors.

Below we fit an MLR and regress to generate its output. Note that we continue using elemapi which is the corrected version.

## Fit MLR 
fit <- lm(api00 ~ ell + meals + yr_rnd + mobility + acs_k3 + acs_46 + 
            full + emer + enroll, data = elemapi2)

## Generate the MLR output
regress(fit)
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>                 Linear Regression Output 
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>          Stats |     Value              Source |      DF         SS        MS
#>  ------------- + ---------          ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>  Number of Obs |       395               Model |       9  6740702.0  748966.9
#>      F(9, 385) |    232.41            Residual |     385  1240707.8    3222.6
#>       Prob > F |   0.00000          ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>      R-Squared |   0.84455               Total |     394  7981409.8   20257.4
#>  Adj R-Squared |   0.84092                                                   
#>            AIC |   4323.62                                 Root MSE     56.77
#>                |                                                             
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>          api00 |     Coef.  Std.Err            t   P>|t| [95% Conf. Interval]
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>            ell |  -0.86007  0.21063        -4.08 0.00005   -1.27291  -0.44723
#>          meals |  -2.94822  0.17035       -17.31 0.00000   -3.28209  -2.61434
#>         yr_rnd | -19.88875  9.25844        -2.15 0.03232  -38.03529  -1.74220
#>       mobility |  -1.30135  0.43621        -2.98 0.00303   -2.15631  -0.44639
#>         acs_k3 |   1.31870  2.25268         0.59 0.55863   -3.09656   5.73396
#>         acs_46 |   2.03246  0.79832         2.55 0.01129    0.46775   3.59717
#>           full |   0.60972  0.47582         1.28 0.20083   -0.32289   1.54232
#>           emer |  -0.70662  0.60541        -1.17 0.24386   -1.89322   0.47998
#>         enroll |  -0.01216  0.01679        -0.72 0.46927   -0.04508   0.02075
#>    (Intercept) | 758.94179 62.28601        12.18 0.00000  636.86122 881.02237
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> (Fit: lm(formula = api00 ~ ell + meals + yr_rnd + mobility + acs_k3 +      acs_4
#> 6 + full + emer + enroll, data = elemapi2))
#> (ell: english language learners)
#> (meals: pct free meals)
#> (yr_rnd: year round school)
#> (mobility: pct 1st year in school)
#> (acs_k3: avg class size k-3)
#> (acs_46: avg class size 4-6)
#> (full: pct full credential)
#> (emer: pct emer credential)
#> (enroll: number of students)
#> (api00: api 2000)

As with the SLR, we check the number of observations first. The analysis included only 395 observations. Next, we check the p-value of the F-test to see if the overall model is significant. With a p-value of zero to four decimal places, the model is statistically significant.

The R-squared is 0.8446, meaning that approximately 84% of the variability of api00 is accounted for by the variables in the model. In this case, the adjusted R-squared indicates that about 84% of the variability of api00 is accounted for by the model, even after taking into account the number of predictor variables in the model. The coefficients for each of the variables indicates the amount of change one could expect in api00 given a one-unit change in the value of that variable, given that all other variables in the model are held constant. For example, consider the variable ell. We would expect a decrease of 0.86 in the api00 score for every one unit increase in ell, assuming that all other variables in the model are held constant.

Finally, the interpretation of much of the output from the multiple regression is the same as it was for the simple regression.

3.3.4.1 Correlation

Another useful step is to see the correlations among the variables in the regression model. You can do this with cor function in base R.

## First select only those variables included in the regression model
elemapi2 %>% 
  ## from dplyr package
  select(api00, ell, meals, yr_rnd, mobility, acs_k3, acs_46, full, 
         emer, enroll) %>% 
  ## By default, we use Pearson's correlation coefficients.
  ## Since there are missing values, we use complete observations.
  cor(use = "complete.obs")
#>               api00         ell      meals      yr_rnd    mobility      acs_k3
#> api00     1.0000000 -0.76551868 -0.9002019 -0.48307238 -0.21059437  0.17121360
#> ell      -0.7655187  1.00000000  0.7710516  0.51037145 -0.01490264 -0.05530898
#> meals    -0.9002019  0.77105158  1.0000000  0.42466164  0.22072063 -0.18880490
#> yr_rnd   -0.4830724  0.51037145  0.4246616  1.00000000  0.03212644  0.02223492
#> mobility -0.2105944 -0.01490264  0.2207206  0.03212644  1.00000000  0.03971234
#> acs_k3    0.1712136 -0.05530898 -0.1888049  0.02223492  0.03971234  1.00000000
#> acs_46    0.2340041 -0.17425264 -0.2137040 -0.04188177  0.12799113  0.27077573
#> full      0.5758949 -0.48669692 -0.5284960 -0.40447509  0.02353604  0.16107575
#> emer     -0.5902011  0.48235017  0.5402404  0.44009046  0.06117041 -0.11111080
#> enroll   -0.3221386  0.41491624  0.2426334  0.59198733  0.10073563  0.10843216
#>               acs_46        full        emer      enroll
#> api00     0.23400410  0.57589495 -0.59020109 -0.32213863
#> ell      -0.17425264 -0.48669692  0.48235017  0.41491624
#> meals    -0.21370399 -0.52849604  0.54024037  0.24263344
#> yr_rnd   -0.04188177 -0.40447509  0.44009046  0.59198733
#> mobility  0.12799113  0.02353604  0.06117041  0.10073563
#> acs_k3    0.27077573  0.16107575 -0.11111080  0.10843216
#> acs_46    1.00000000  0.12117885 -0.12832477  0.02807765
#> full      0.12117885  1.00000000 -0.90590415 -0.33840501
#> emer     -0.12832477 -0.90590415  1.00000000  0.34170063
#> enroll    0.02807765 -0.33840501  0.34170063  1.00000000
We see `meals` and `ell` have the two strongest correlations with api00. These correlations are negative, meaning that as the value of one variable goes down, the value of the other variable tends to go up. Knowing that these variables are strongly associated with `api00`, we might predict that they would be statistically significant predictor variables in the regression model.

3.3.4.2 Transforming variables

Earlier we focus on screening the data for potential errors. Here we look at the issue of normality before moving on to the regression diagnostics to verify the required assumptions of linear regression.

Some researchers believe that linear regression requires that the outcome (dependent) and predictor variables be normally distributed. We need to clarify this issue. In actuality, it is the residuals that need to be normally distributed.  The residuals need to be normal only for the t-tests to be valid. The estimation of the regression coefficients does not require normally distributed residuals. As we are interested in having valid t-tests, we will investigate issues concerning normality.

A common cause of non-normally distributed residuals is non-normally distributed outcome and/or predictor variables.

Another option is to check the normal quantile plot which plots the quantiles of a variable against the quantiles of a normal (Gaussian) distribution. The qnorm is sensitive to non-normality near the tails, and indeed we see considerable deviations from normal, the diagonal line, in the tails. This plot is typical of variables that are strongly skewed to the right.

Below we make a histogram, boxplot, and normal quantile plot of enroll. By default, the function superimposes a normal curve on the histogram.

## Set up graph parameter
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))

## Histogram of enroll
histogram(elemapi2, enroll, 
          main = "Histogram of enroll", 
          xlab = "Number of students")
#> (A histogram is drawn for 'enroll' with overlay normal curve)

## Boxplot enroll
elemapi2 %$% 
  boxplot(enroll, 
          main = "Boxplot of enroll", 
          xlab = "Number of students")

## Normal quantile plot
elemapi2 %$%
  qqnorm(enroll, 
          main = "Normal Quantile Plot of enroll", 
          xlab = "Number of students")
## Add a diagonal line 
elemapi2 %$% 
  qqline(enroll, col = "red")


par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

The distribution looks a bit right-skewed and the Shapiro-Wilk test of normality shows a significance, meaning that the data is not normally distributed.

Now we are fairly convinced that enroll is not normally distributed. How should we address this problem? First, we may try entering the variable as it is into the regression, but if we see problems, which we likely would, then we may try to transform enroll to make it more normally distributed. Potential transformations include taking the log, the square root, or raising the variable to a power. Selecting the appropriate transformation is somewhat of an art.

The mStats has the function ladder to help the process.

## Check ladder transformation of enroll
ladder(elemapi2, enroll)
#> _____________________________________________________ 
#> 
#> Ladder of Transformation 
#> _____________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>     Transformation |          formula       W P-Value
#>  ----------------- + ---------------- ------- -------
#>               cube |         enroll^3 0.44878 0.00000
#>              squre |         enroll^2 0.68913 0.00000
#>                raw |           enroll 0.91381 0.00000
#>        square-root |     sqrt(enroll) 0.97761 0.00001
#>                log |      log(enroll) 0.99631 0.48502
#>    reciprocal root | 1 / sqrt(enroll) 0.97034 0.00000
#>         reciprocal |       1 / enroll 0.90639 0.00000
#>  reciprocal square |   1 / (enroll^2) 0.71188 0.00000
#>    reciprocal cube |   1 / (enroll^3) 0.51161 0.00000
#>  ----------------- + ---------------- ------- -------
#> _____________________________________________________

The log transform has the smallest W statistics and non-significant p-value from the Shapiro-Wilk test of normality.

Let’s use generate and create a new variable to transform into log scale.

## Generate log enroll 
elemapi2 <- generate(elemapi2, lenroll, log(enroll))
#> ('lenroll' generated 400 valid values)

## Check log enroll in histogram 
histogram(elemapi2, lenroll, 
          xlab = "Number of students on log scale")

#> (A histogram is drawn for 'lenroll' with overlay normal curve)

3.3.4.3 Exercise

  1. Make graphs of api99: histogram, boxplot, and normal quantile plot.
  2. What is the correlation between api99 and meals?
  3. Regress api99 on meals. What does the output tell you?
  4. Create and list the fitted (predicted) values.
  5. Graph meals and api99 with and without the regression line.
  6. Look at the correlations among the variables api99 meals ell avg_ed using cor.
  7. Make a scatterplot matrix for these variables and relate the correlation results to the scatterplot matrix.
  8. Perform a regression predicting api99 from meals and ell. Interpret the output. ***

3.3.5 Model Diagnostics

So far, we have learned how to run linear regression in R, with some methods of checking the data. Unless we verify that our data meet the assumptions under linear regression, the results may be misleading. In this section, we will explore how we can use R to check whether our data satisfy the assumptions. This section was based on UCLA’s STATA tutorial series.4

We will focus on the following assumptions.

- Linearity 
      The relationships between the predictors and the outcome variable should be linear.
- Normality
      The errors should be normally distributed – technically normality is necessary only for hypothesis tests to be valid, estimation of the coefficients only requires that the errors be identically and independently distributed
- Homogeneity of variance (homoscedasticity) 
      The error variance should be constant
- Independence 
      The errors associated with one observation are not correlated with the errors of any other observation.

In this section, we use an example dataset called crime. This dataset is from Statistical Methods for Social Sciences, Third Edition by Alan Agresti, and Barbara Finlay (Prentice Hall, 1997). Detail can be read here.5

Variables

  1. sid - id
  2. state - state name
  3. crime - violent crimes per 100,000 people
  4. murder - murders per 1,000,000
  5. pctmetro - the percentage of the population living in metropolitan areas
  6. pctwhite - the percentage of the white population
  7. pcths - the percentage of the population with high school education and above
  8. poverty - the percentage of the population living under the poverty line
  9. single - the percentage of the population that are single parents

We want to predict crime based on pctmetro, poverty, and single. That is to say, we want to build a linear regression model between the response variable crime and the independent variables pctmetro, poverty, and single.

First, let’s import this dataset into R.

## Import crime dataset
crime <- haven::read_dta("https://stats.idre.ucla.edu/stat/stata/webbooks/reg/crime.dta")

## Check the dataset
codebook(crime)
#> ________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#> Codebook: 'crime' 
#> ________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>  No Variable |              Label      Type Obs_Num <NA> <NA>(%)
#>  -- -------- + ------------------ --------- ------- ---- -------
#>   1      sid |               <NA>   numeric      51    0       0
#>   2    state |               <NA> character      51    0       0
#>   3    crime | violent crime rate   numeric      51    0       0
#>   4   murder |        murder rate   numeric      51    0       0
#>   5 pctmetro |   pct metropolitan   numeric      51    0       0
#>   6 pctwhite |          pct white   numeric      51    0       0
#>   7    pcths |   pct hs graduates   numeric      51    0       0
#>   8  poverty |        pct poverty   numeric      51    0       0
#>   9   single |  pct single parent   numeric      51    0       0
#>  -- -------- + ------------------ --------- ------- ---- -------
#> ________________________________________________________________ 
#> (Dataset Label: crime data from agresti & finlay - 1997)

3.3.5.1 Influential data

Often you deal with a dataset where a single observation can make a huge difference in the regression output if it is substantially different from all other observations. In this case, you need to investigate this further. There are three ways that observations can be unusually different.

  1. Outliers

They are large residuals in linear regression. An outlier often represents data entry error.

  1. Leverage points

An extreme value in a predictor variable is called high leverage point.

  1. Influential points

If removing such extreme values or high leverage points substantially changes the coefficient estimates, such values are called influential points.

Let’s create a scatter plot matrix on variables, crime, pctmetro, poverty, and single.

## Scatter plot matrices
crime %>% 
  select(crime, pctmetro, poverty, single) %>%
  scatterPlotMatrix(main = "Scatter Plot Matrices of Crime Dataset")

We see a data point that is far away from the rest of the data in every plot. Let’s make individual plots so that we can get a better view. We also add labels to these data points so that we know which state these data points come from.

Scatter plots with data labels

Below we use text function from base R to add labels to the data points. Check the help page by running ?text.

## Set graphs parameter
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))

## Plot crime and pctmetro
plot(crime ~ pctmetro, data = crime, main = "crime ~ pctmetro")
crime %$% text(crime ~ pctmetro, labels = state, 
               cex = 0.8, pos = 1, col = "blue")

## Plot crime and poverty
plot(crime ~ poverty, data = crime, main = "crime ~ poverty")
crime %$% text(crime ~ poverty, labels = state,
               cex = 0.8, pos = 1, col = "blue")

## Plot crime and single
plot(crime ~ single, data = crime, main = "crime ~ single")
crime %$% text(crime ~ single, labels = state,
               cex = 0.8, pos = 1, col = "blue")

## Reset graphs parameter
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

We see the data point that is far away from the rest of the data comes from DC state. Since it stands out, we can say this is an outlier and leverage point. We keep this in mind while we continue fitting the linear model.

Fitting a linear model

Let’s fit the model and run regress.

## Fit the model 
fit <- lm(crime ~ pctmetro + poverty + single, data = crime)

## Generate regression output
## Set decimal point to 2 to make the output fit the page width
reg <- regress(fit, rnd = 2)
#> _________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>                 Linear Regression Output 
#> _________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>          Stats |    Value             Source |    DF         SS        MS
#>  ------------- + --------         ---------- + ----- ---------- ---------
#>  Number of Obs |       51              Model |     3  8170480.2 2723493.4
#>       F(3, 47) |    82.16           Residual |    47  1557994.5   33148.8
#>       Prob > F |     0.00         ---------- + ----- ---------- ---------
#>      R-Squared |     0.84              Total |    50  9728474.7  194569.5
#>  Adj R-Squared |     0.83                                                
#>            AIC |   681.41                              Root MSE    182.07
#>                |                                                         
#>  ------------- + -------- ------- ------------ ----- ---------- ---------
#>          crime |    Coef. Std.Err            t P>|t| [95% Conf. Interval]
#>  ------------- + -------- ------- ------------ ----- ---------- ---------
#>       pctmetro |     7.83    1.25         6.24  0.00       5.37     10.29
#>        poverty |    17.68    6.94         2.55  0.01       4.08     31.28
#>         single |   132.41   15.50         8.54  0.00     102.02    162.79
#>    (Intercept) | -1666.44  147.85       -11.27  0.00   -1956.23  -1376.65
#>  ------------- + -------- ------- ------------ ----- ---------- ---------
#> _________________________________________________________________________ 
#> (Fit: lm(formula = crime ~ pctmetro + poverty + single, data = crime))
#> (pctmetro: pct metropolitan)
#> (poverty: pct poverty)
#> (single: pct single parent)
#> (crime: violent crime rate)

Next, we are going to generate studentized residuals, leverage values, and Cook’s distance values from the model to identify the potentially unusual or influential points.

Below we use predict function to generate the data with these statistics, and store the result to rstd.

## Generate diagnostic statistics
rstd <- predict(reg)

## Check the dataset
codebook(rstd)
#> _____________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#> Codebook: 'rstd' 
#> _____________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>  No  Variable |                  Label      Type Obs_Num <NA> <NA>(%)
#>  -- --------- + ---------------------- --------- ------- ---- -------
#>   1     crime |                   <NA>   numeric      51    0       0
#>   2  pctmetro |                   <NA>   numeric      51    0       0
#>   3   poverty |                   <NA>   numeric      51    0       0
#>   4    single |                   <NA>   numeric      51    0       0
#>   5       sid |                   <NA>   numeric      51    0       0
#>   6     state |                   <NA> character      51    0       0
#>   7    murder |                   <NA>   numeric      51    0       0
#>   8  pctwhite |                   <NA>   numeric      51    0       0
#>   9     pcths |                   <NA>   numeric      51    0       0
#>  10    fitted |          Fitted values   numeric      51    0       0
#>  11     resid |              Residuals   numeric      51    0       0
#>  12 std.resid |  Studentized Residuals   numeric      51    0       0
#>  13       hat | Leverage or hat values   numeric      51    0       0
#>  14     sigma |              hat sigma   numeric      51    0       0
#>  15    cooksd |        Cook's Distance   numeric      51    0       0
#>  -- --------- + ---------------------- --------- ------- ---- -------
#> _____________________________________________________________________

The studentized residuals are stored under the variable, std.resid, leverage under hat and Cook’s distance under cooksd.

Boxplots for residuals

Let’s check the studentized residuals with boxplot.

## Set graphs parameter
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))

## Boxplot 
rstd %$% boxplot(std.resid, main = "Studentized Residuals")
rstd %$% boxplot(std.resid, main = "Leverage values")
rstd %$% boxplot(std.resid, main = "Cook's Distance")


## Set graphs parameter
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Studentized Residuals

We want to see which state has studentized residuals over +2 or under -2. We do this by listing a few lines from the top and the bottom. We use head and tail functions from base R to get top and bottom rows.

## Check top rows
rstd %>% 
  arrange(std.resid) %>% 
  select(sid, state, std.resid) %>% 
  head
#>   sid state std.resid
#> 1  25    ms -3.193796
#> 2  18    la -1.793716
#> 3  39    ri -1.653521
#> 4  47    wa -1.294313
#> 5  35    oh -1.144457
#> 6  48    wi -1.126045

## Check bottom rows
rstd %>% 
  arrange(std.resid) %>% 
  select(sid, state, std.resid) %>% 
  tail
#>    sid state std.resid
#> 46  16    ks  1.074898
#> 47  14    il  1.147723
#> 48  13    id  1.284314
#> 49  12    ia  1.564434
#> 50   9    fl  2.470016
#> 51  51    dc  3.327972

We should pay attention to studentized residuals that exceed +2 or -2, and get even more concerned about residuals that exceed +2.5 or -2.5 and even yet more concerned about residuals that exceed +3 or -3. These results show that DC and MS are the most worrisome observations followed by FL.

Below we use abs function to get absolute values of studentized residuals that are greater than 2.

rstd %>% 
  filter(abs(std.resid) > 2) %>% 
  select(sid, state, std.resid, crime, pctmetro, poverty, single)
#>   sid state std.resid crime pctmetro poverty single
#> 1   9    fl  2.470016  1206     93.0    17.8   10.6
#> 2  51    dc  3.327972  2922    100.0    26.4   22.1
#> 3  25    ms -3.193796   434     30.7    24.7   14.7

We see the data for the three potential outliers we identified, namely Florida, Mississippi, and Washington D.C. Looking carefully at these three observations, we couldn’t find any data entry error, though we may want to do another regression analysis with the extreme point such as DC deleted.

Leverage value

Below we also list 6 largest values of leverage or hat values along with variables in the regression model.

## List 6 largest leverage values 
rstd %>% 
  arrange(hat) %>% 
  select(sid, state, hat, crime, pctmetro, poverty, single) %>% 
  tail
#>    sid state       hat crime pctmetro poverty single
#> 46  46    vt 0.1362576   114     27.0    10.0   11.0
#> 47  18    la 0.1652769  1062     75.0    26.4   14.9
#> 48  49    wv 0.1802005   208     41.8    22.2    9.4
#> 49  25    ms 0.1910120   434     30.7    24.7   14.7
#> 50   1    ak 0.2606759   761     41.8     9.1   14.3
#> 51  51    dc 0.5363830  2922    100.0    26.4   22.1

The state DC also has the largest leverage values.

As a rule of thumb, a data point with leverage value greater than (2k + 2) / n should be carefully checked, where k is the number of predictors and n is the number of observations. In our case, we got 3 predictors and 51 observations, hence (2 * 3 + 2) / 51 = 0.1568627.

## List variables in the regression model if leverage > 0.156
rstd %>% 
  filter(hat > 0.156) %>% 
  arrange(hat) %>% 
  select(sid, state, hat, crime, pctmetro, poverty, single)
#>   sid state       hat crime pctmetro poverty single
#> 1  18    la 0.1652769  1062     75.0    26.4   14.9
#> 2  49    wv 0.1802005   208     41.8    22.2    9.4
#> 3  25    ms 0.1910120   434     30.7    24.7   14.7
#> 4   1    ak 0.2606759   761     41.8     9.1   14.3
#> 5  51    dc 0.5363830  2922    100.0    26.4   22.1

Similar to the studentized residuals, DC has the largest leverage value.

We can also plot the leverage by the studentized residuals to look for observations that are high on both measures. The points we would examine are values in the upper right or lower right corners, which are outside the red dashed Cook’s distance line. These are points that would be influential in the model and removing them would likely noticeably alter the regression results. This is a quick way of checking potential influential observations and outliers at the same time. Both types of points are of great concern to us.

Below we create the graph by inputting reg into plot function.

## Leverage versus residuals plot
plot(reg)

We see two data points with row numbers 51 and 25 are outside the Cook’s distance which is indicated by two red dashed lines. They need to be checked carefully. As it turns out, row 51 and 25 corresponds to the state, DC and MS.

## List variables if state == dc or ms: note R is case-sensitive 
rstd %>% 
  filter(state == "dc" | state == "ms") %>% 
  select(sid, state, std.resid, hat, crime, pctmetro, poverty, single)
#>   sid state std.resid      hat crime pctmetro poverty single
#> 1  51    dc  3.327972 0.536383  2922    100.0    26.4   22.1
#> 2  25    ms -3.193796 0.191012   434     30.7    24.7   14.7

Cook’s Distance

Let’s move on to Cook’s distance, which combines information on the residual and leverage. The lowest possible value is zero, and the higher the Cook’Distance is, the more influential the point is. The conventional cut-off point is 4 / n.

Below we list all observations with the Cook’s distance that is greater than 4 / 51.

## List all variables if Cook's D > 4 / 51 
rstd %>% 
  filter(cooksd > (4 / 51)) %>% 
  select(sid, state, std.resid, cooksd, hat, crime, pctmetro, poverty, single)
#>   sid state std.resid    cooksd       hat crime pctmetro poverty single
#> 1  18    la -1.793716 0.1592638 0.1652769  1062     75.0    26.4   14.9
#> 2   9    fl  2.470016 0.1736290 0.1022025  1206     93.0    17.8   10.6
#> 3  51    dc  3.327972 3.2034289 0.5363830  2922    100.0    26.4   22.1
#> 4  25    ms -3.193796 0.6021060 0.1910120   434     30.7    24.7   14.7

The DC’s Cook’s Distance is by far the largest.

avPlots

Finally, we create added-variable plots to identify influential points. These are also called partial-regression plots since they help us to evaluate the residuals of a predictor variable while holding the other predictors constant.

Below we use avPlots from car package.

## Install car pacakage if you don't have it. 
install.packages("car")
## Create avPlots by inputting our model fit
car::avPlots(fit)

In the last avplot for single, the graph shows crime by single after both crime and single has been adjusted for all other predictors in the model. The line plotted has the same slope as the coefficient for single. This plot shows how the observation for DC influences the coefficient. You can see how the regression line is tugged upwards trying to fit through the extreme value of DC (51). Alaska (1) and West Virginia (the lowest data point) may also exert substantial leverage on the coefficient of single.

DC has appeared as an outlier as well as an influential point in every analysis. Since DC is not a state, we can use this to justify omitting it from the analysis saying that we wish to just analyze states.

Let’s run regress by removing the state DC.

## Remove dc row
crime2 <- crime %>%
  filter(state != "dc")

## Fit linear model using dataset with dc row removed
fit <-  lm(crime ~ pctmetro + poverty + single, data = crime2)

## Regression report with 2 decimal points
regress(fit, rnd = 2)
#> _________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>                 Linear Regression Output 
#> _________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>          Stats |    Value             Source |    DF         SS        MS
#>  ------------- + --------         ---------- + ----- ---------- ---------
#>  Number of Obs |       50              Model |     3  3098767.1 1032922.4
#>       F(3, 46) |    39.90           Residual |    46  1190858.1   25888.2
#>       Prob > F |     0.00         ---------- + ----- ---------- ---------
#>      R-Squared |     0.72              Total |    49  4289625.2   87543.4
#>  Adj R-Squared |     0.70                                                
#>            AIC |   655.80                              Root MSE    160.90
#>                |                                                         
#>  ------------- + -------- ------- ------------ ----- ---------- ---------
#>          crime |    Coef. Std.Err            t P>|t| [95% Conf. Interval]
#>  ------------- + -------- ------- ------------ ----- ---------- ---------
#>       pctmetro |     7.71    1.11         6.95  0.00       5.54      9.89
#>        poverty |    18.28    6.14         2.98  0.00       6.26     30.31
#>         single |    89.40   17.84         5.01  0.00      54.44    124.36
#>    (Intercept) | -1197.54  180.49        -6.64  0.00   -1551.29   -843.78
#>  ------------- + -------- ------- ------------ ----- ---------- ---------
#> _________________________________________________________________________ 
#> (Fit: lm(formula = crime ~ pctmetro + poverty + single, data = crime2))
#> (pctmetro: pct metropolitan)
#> (poverty: pct poverty)
#> (single: pct single parent)
#> (crime: violent crime rate)

3.3.5.2 Normality

Normality of residuals is only required for the validity of hypothesis testing so that p-values for t-test and F-test will be valid. It is not required to get unbiased estimates of the regression coefficients.

OLS regression merely requires that the residuals (errors) be identically and independently distributed. Furthermore, there is no assumption or requirement that the predictor variables be normally distributed. If this were the case then we would not be able to use dummy coded variables in our models.

We use the elemapi2 data we imported in the previous section on Simple Linear Regression. Let’s fit a model for academic performance (api00) using three predictors: percentage of students receiving free meals (meals), percentage of English language learners (ell), and percentage of teachers with emergency credentials (emer).

## Fit the model 
fit <- lm(api00 ~ meals + ell + emer, data = elemapi2)

## Generate regression output
reg <- regress(fit)
#> ____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>                 Linear Regression Output 
#> ____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>          Stats |     Value             Source |      DF         SS        MS
#>  ------------- + ---------         ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>  Number of Obs |       400              Model |       3  6749782.7 2249927.6
#>      F(3, 396) |    673.00           Residual |     396  1323889.3    3343.2
#>       Prob > F |   0.00000         ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>      R-Squared |   0.83602              Total |     399  8073672.0   20234.8
#>  Adj R-Squared |   0.83478                                                  
#>            AIC |   4387.00                                Root MSE     57.82
#>                |                                                            
#>  ------------- + --------- ------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>          api00 |     Coef. Std.Err            t   P>|t| [95% Conf. Interval]
#>  ------------- + --------- ------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>          meals |  -3.15919 0.14974       -21.10 0.00000   -3.45267  -2.86570
#>            ell |  -0.90987 0.18464        -4.93 0.00000   -1.27178  -0.54797
#>           emer |  -1.57350 0.29311        -5.37 0.00000   -2.14800  -0.99900
#>    (Intercept) | 886.70326 6.25976       141.65 0.00000  874.43414 898.97239
#>  ------------- + --------- ------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#> ____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> (Fit: lm(formula = api00 ~ meals + ell + emer, data = elemapi2))
#> (meals: pct free meals)
#> (ell: english language learners)
#> (emer: pct emer credential)
#> (api00: api 2000)

There are two ways to check the normality of residuals: Normal quantile plot and Shapiro-Wilk test of normality.

Below we create a normal quantile plot by inputting reg into plot function.

## Plots for linear model diagnostics
plot(reg)

We have seen this plot matrix when we discussed MLR. The second plot is the normal quantile plot. If the data points should fall nicely along the dashed line, we say the residuals are normally distributed.

Next, we run a numerical test on residual data by summ funtion. We generate residuals data by inputting reg into predict function. The residuals are stored in the variable resid.

## Generate residuals
rstd <- predict(reg)

## summary statistic on `resid` variable
summ(rstd, resid)
#> ___________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#> Summary 
#> ___________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>  Variables | Obs. NA. Mean Std.Dev Median    Q1   Q3    Min   Max Normality
#>  --------- + ---- --- ---- ------- ------ ----- ---- ------ ----- ---------
#>      resid |  400   0 -0.0    57.6   -3.7 -40.0 36.4 -185.5 178.5     0.510
#>  --------- + ---- --- ---- ------- ------ ----- ---- ------ ----- ---------
#> ___________________________________________________________________________ 
#> (resid: Residuals)

Look at the p-value under the column Normality. Since the p-value is very large (0.51), we conclude that the residual is normally distributed. This is consistent with that from the normal quantile plot described above.

3.3.5.3 Homoscedasticity

One of the main assumptions of the linear regression is the homoscedasticity of residuals. It means that the variances of residuals are constant across the data points. If the residuals are plotted against fitted values, they should not show any pattern. A commonly used graphical method is to plot the residuals versus fitted values (or predicted values).

We have already seen this plot in the plot matrix above. It is the first plot.

Below we create this plot from the raw data of predict function.

## plot residuals versus fitted values
plot(resid ~ fitted, data = rstd, 
     main = "Residuals versus Fitted values", 
     xlab = "Fitted values",
     ylab = "Residuals")
## Add a horizontal red line to indicate 0 
abline(h = 0, col = "red")

We see that the pattern of the data points is getting a little narrower towards the right end, which is an indication of heteroscedasticity.

Now let’s look at the Breusch-Pagan test for heteroskedasticity.

## Perform  the Breusch-Pagan test
hettest(reg)
#> _______________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>           Breusch-Pagan test for heteroskedasticity 
#> _______________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>    Null Hypothesis |              Variables Chi2(1) Prob > Chi2
#>  ----------------- + ---------------------- ------- -----------
#>  Constant variance | Fitted values of api00    8.89     0.03080
#>  ----------------- + ---------------------- ------- -----------
#> _______________________________________________________________

## Studentized version 
hettest(reg, studentize = TRUE)
#> _______________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>     Studentized Breusch-Pagan test for heteroskedasticity 
#> _______________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>    Null Hypothesis |              Variables Chi2(1) Prob > Chi2
#>  ----------------- + ---------------------- ------- -----------
#>  Constant variance | Fitted values of api00    8.39     0.03858
#>  ----------------- + ---------------------- ------- -----------
#> _______________________________________________________________

if the p-value is very small, we would have to reject the hypothesis and accept the alternative hypothesis that the variance is not homogenous. So in this case, the evidence is against the null hypothesis that the variance is homogeneous. These tests are very sensitive to model assumptions, such as the assumption of normality. Therefore it is a common practice to combine the tests with diagnostic plots to make a judgment on the severity of the heteroscedasticity and to decide if any correction is needed for heteroscedasticity. In our case, the plot above does not show too strong an evidence.

3.3.5.4 Multicollinearity

When there is a perfect linear relationship among the predictors, the estimates for a regression model cannot be uniquely computed. If two variables are near perfect linear combinations of one another and there are more than two variables involved, it is often called multicollinearity.

As the degree of multicollinearity increases, the regression model estimates of the coefficients become unstable and the standard errors for the coefficients can get wildly inflated. It means the estimates for the regression coefficients are biased and the conclusion will be incorrect.

Below we use vif function from the car package by inputting the model fit to predict api00 from meals, ell, and emer.

The vif stands for variance inflation factor. As a rule of thumb, a variable whose VIF values are greater than 10 may merit further investigation. Tolerance, defined as 1/VIF, is used by many researchers to check on the degree of collinearity. A tolerance value lower than 0.1 is comparable to a VIF of 10. It means that the variable could be considered as a linear combination of other independent variables.

## vif for the model fit
car::vif(fit)
#>    meals      ell     emer 
#> 2.725056 2.510512 1.414817

The VIFs look fine here.

Here is an example where the VIFs are more worrisome.

## Fit another model
fit <- lm(api00 ~ acs_k3 + avg_ed + grad_sch + col_grad + some_col, data = elemapi2)

## regression report
regress(fit)
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>                 Linear Regression Output 
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>          Stats |     Value              Source |      DF         SS        MS
#>  ------------- + ---------          ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>  Number of Obs |       379               Model |       5  5056268.5 1011253.7
#>      F(5, 373) |    143.79            Residual |     373  2623191.2    7032.7
#>       Prob > F |   0.00000          ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>      R-Squared |   0.65841               Total |     378  7679459.8   20316.0
#>  Adj R-Squared |   0.65384                                                   
#>            AIC |   4440.81                                 Root MSE     83.86
#>                |                                                             
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>          api00 |     Coef.  Std.Err            t   P>|t| [95% Conf. Interval]
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>         acs_k3 |  11.45725  3.27541         3.50 0.00053    5.03745  17.87706
#>         avg_ed | 227.26382 37.21960         6.11 0.00000  154.31341 300.21423
#>       grad_sch |  -2.09090  1.35229        -1.55 0.12291   -4.74139   0.55959
#>       col_grad |  -2.96783  1.01781        -2.92 0.00376   -4.96274  -0.97292
#>       some_col |  -0.76045  0.81097        -0.94 0.34900   -2.34995   0.82904
#>    (Intercept) | -82.60913 81.84638        -1.01 0.31348 -243.02804  77.80978
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> (Fit: lm(formula = api00 ~ acs_k3 + avg_ed + grad_sch + col_grad +      some_col
#> , data = elemapi2))
#> (acs_k3: avg class size k-3)
#> (avg_ed: avg parent ed)
#> (grad_sch: parent grad school)
#> (col_grad: parent college grad)
#> (some_col: parent some college)
#> (api00: api 2000)

## Check vif 
car::vif(fit)
#>    acs_k3    avg_ed  grad_sch  col_grad  some_col 
#>  1.028947 43.570329 14.864590 14.778836  4.065150

In this example, the VIFvalues for avg_ed, grad_sch and col_grad are worrisome. All of these variables measure the education of the parents and the very high VIF values indicate that these variables are possibly redundant. For example, after you know grad_sch and col_grad, you probably can predict avg_ed very well. In this example, multicollinearity arises because we have put in too many variables that measure the same thing, parent education.

Let’s omit one of the parent education variables, avg_ed.

## Fit another model: omit avg_ed
fit <- lm(api00 ~ acs_k3 + grad_sch + col_grad + some_col, data = elemapi2)

## regression report
regress(fit)
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>                 Linear Regression Output 
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> 
#>          Stats |     Value              Source |      DF         SS        MS
#>  ------------- + ---------          ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>  Number of Obs |       398               Model |       4  4180144.3 1045036.1
#>      F(4, 393) |    107.12            Residual |     393  3834062.8    9755.9
#>       Prob > F |   0.00000          ---------- + ------- ---------- ---------
#>      R-Squared |   0.52159               Total |     397  8014207.1   20186.9
#>  Adj R-Squared |   0.51672                                                   
#>            AIC |   4792.32                                 Root MSE     98.77
#>                |                                                             
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>          api00 |     Coef.  Std.Err            t   P>|t| [95% Conf. Interval]
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#>         acs_k3 |  11.71260  3.66487         3.20 0.00151    4.52945  18.89575
#>       grad_sch |   5.63476  0.45820        12.30 0.00000    4.73669   6.53283
#>       col_grad |   2.47992  0.33955         7.30 0.00000    1.81439   3.14544
#>       some_col |   2.15827  0.44388         4.86 0.00000    1.28826   3.02828
#>    (Intercept) | 283.74462 70.32475         4.03 0.00007  145.90810 421.58113
#>  ------------- + --------- -------- ------------ ------- ---------- ---------
#> _____________________________________________________________________________ 
#> (Fit: lm(formula = api00 ~ acs_k3 + grad_sch + col_grad + some_col,      data = 
#> elemapi2))
#> (acs_k3: avg class size k-3)
#> (grad_sch: parent grad school)
#> (col_grad: parent college grad)
#> (some_col: parent some college)
#> (api00: api 2000)

## Check vif 
car::vif(fit)
#>   acs_k3 grad_sch col_grad some_col 
#> 1.023891 1.262417 1.277586 1.034451
